image.title: DW
image.description: News, Analysis and Service from Germany and Europe - in 30 Languages

item.title: Germany prepares for mission in the Strait of Hormuz
item.description: The German minesweeper Fulda has set course for the Mediterranean. The German Armed Forces may assist in clearing mines in the Strait of Hormuz — although only under certain conditions.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Iran war: US says ceasefire still holds despite clashes
item.description: The US military has again warned it will protect commercial shipping against Iranian attack in the Strait of Hormuz. However, US officials underlined that the ceasefire is still in place.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Venice Biennale in crisis: The controversies explained
item.description: The traditional Golden Lion will not be awarded this year, following the resignation of the jury over Russia's and Israel's disputed participation.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Germany: BioNTech shutting down COVID vaccine production entirely, 1,860 jobs at risk
item.description: The German company that pioneered one of the first mRNA coronavirus vaccines is halting production, handing the reins to partner Pfizer. BioNTech is restructuring to focus on research and illnesses like cancer.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Japan pivots Indo-Pacific strategy toward Southeast Asia
item.description: Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi used a visit to Vietnam to update Japan's Indo-Pacific strategy, putting energy security, critical minerals and ASEAN resilience at its center.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Germany: Difficult first year for Chancellor Friedrich Merz
item.description: A year after taking office, Germany's federal leadership is struggling with the drudgery of coalition governance. Whenever there are problems, the coalition partners are blamed. Or US President Donald Trump.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: What is Hantavirus?
item.description: Three people have died on a cruise ship in the Atlantic, reportedly from hantavirus. The disease has been confirmed in another traveler. What are the symptoms of the virus, and how does infection occur?
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Romania's government collapses after Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan loses confidence vote
item.description: Prime Minister Ilie Bolojan has been voted out of office by lawmakers. Romania's coalition was driven apart by unpopular austerity measures, with the Social Democrats leaving and moving to oust the PM.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany: Couple admits €1.9 million parking meter thefts
item.description: A German couple accused of siphoning large quantities of cash from parking meters has confessed in court. Nearly two million euros disappeared from machines in a picturesque Bavarian town — but no one noticed for years.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Decoding China: Beijing courts Europe amid US dysfunction
item.description: China is hoping to secure new allies for a future multipolar world order as the partnership between the US and the European powers slides into a crisis.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: The sea that is vanishing in real time
item.description: The world's largest inland body of water is retreating at an alarming speed. From stranded buildings to vanishing habitats, scientists warn the Caspian Sea may be approaching a tipping point.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Colombia says 9 miners dead after explosion
item.description: Colombia said at least nine workers were dead following a coal mine explosion in the mining town of Sutatausa, about 72 kilometers north of Bogota.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: From India to Iran: How Hitler redefined 'Aryan' for the Nazis
item.description: According to Nazi ideology, an ideal "Aryan" was blond, blue-eyed with athletic features. The term is still tied to Nazi Germany, but its origin lies elsewhere.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Germany news: Leipzig ramming suspect to appear in court
item.description: A man is set to appear before a judge after a car plowed into a busy shopping area, leaving two people dead. DW has the latest.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Malawi fuel crisis deepens as oil shortages spread
item.description: The fuel crisis has hit Malawi probably harder than anywhere else in Africa. The government has been forced to sell off precious gold reserves to buy fuel, but there is a severe cost to this, too.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: India news: West Bengal wakes to a new political reality
item.description: After years of making steady inroads, Narendra Modi's Bharatiya Janata Party has clinched West Bengal at the polls. Meanwhile, India's Foreign Ministry criticized Iran over an attack on the UAE.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Taiwan's Lai returns from Eswatini trip amid China objection
item.description: Taiwan's President Lai Ching-te has arrived home from the tiny African kingdom of Eswatini, after three China-friendly countries denied him permission to fly through their airspace.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: China: Explosion at fireworks factory kills at least 26
item.description: A blast at a fireworks factory in China has killed 26 people and wounded 61 others. President Xi Jinping has called for a thorough probe.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Iran war: UAE reports first Iranian missile and drone attacks in weeks
item.description: The United Arab Emirates has reported missile and drone attacks from Iran, the first of their kind since the April ceasefire. This comes as the US seeks to help ships through the Strait of Hormuz.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Person shot near Washington Monument, US Secret Service says
item.description: The White House was briefly locked down on Monday after the US Secret Service said an individual was shot by law enforcement near the Washington Monument.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Russia offers Ukraine May 8-9 ceasefire amid WWII anniversary, but threatens major reprisals in same breath
item.description: President Putin has unilaterally declared a two-day truce amid commemorations of the end of World War II in Moscow. Ukraine's President Zelenskyy proposed his own truce starting a couple of days earlier.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Dubai's image as a financial hub faces its biggest test yet
item.description: Amid the smoldering Iran war, wealthy elites are quietly shifting capital and other assets out of Dubai and heading to Singapore and Switzerland. Just how resilient is the emirate's safe-haven reputation?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Why Saudi Arabia is withdrawing from sport
item.description: There are few major sports left that are not supported by investment from Saudi Arabia. But with the kingdom pulling out of golf and canceling planned events and contracts elsewhere, is the Saudi sports era over?
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Georg Baselitz, artist renowned for his upside-down paintings, dies
item.description: He was thrown out of art school at the age of 18 because of his love for Picasso. The German artist who never lost his rebellious streak has died at the age of 88.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Angine de Poitrine: A masked rock band conquers the internet
item.description: From underground experiment to sold-out international concerts within days — as the French-Canadian band Angine de Poitrine starts touring the world, we take a look at the viral phenomenon.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Women's Champions League: Bayern fall to Barcelona in semis
item.description: Bayern Munich have come up short in their bid to win the Women's Champions League for the first time after losing to Barcelona in the semifinals.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: How Serbia's government dominates the country's media
item.description: Since Serbia's ruling SNS party came to power in 2012, it has tightened its grip on the media. With an election expected soon, experts fear authorities will try to eliminate the last pockets of independent reporting.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Germany news: Majority thinks coalition government doomed — survey
item.description: More than half of people in Germany believe the coalition government will collapse before its term is up. The head of a leading economic think tank says US tariffs could trigger a German recession.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Millions sway as free Shakira concert lights up Copacabana
item.description: The Colombian superstar delivers one of the biggest shows of her career, as fans flock from Brazil and beyond for her "Women No Longer Cry" world tour.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Where Germany's economy still excels
item.description: German industry is being lapped in key sectors like solar panels, semiconductors and even cars — but the country's midsize businesses suggest the economy isn't losing its edge.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Why Russia's Northern Sea Route is a risk for global trade
item.description: Russia wants its Northern Sea Route to become an important artery of global trade. However, the Arctic shortcut between Europe and Asia is fraught with both political and environmental hurdles.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Austria: Police arrest suspect over poisoned HiPP baby food
item.description: A 39-year-old man has been arrested in Austria for his alleged involvement in an attempt to blackmail a German baby food manufacturer.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Why Ukraine and Israel are arguing over grain
item.description: Cargo ships have docked in Haifa, Israel, carrying grain thought to originate from Russian-occupied parts of Ukraine. Kyiv considers the cargo to be stolen goods — but Israel says evidence is lacking.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Alex Zanardi, F1 driver and multiple CART and Paralympic champion, dies at 59
item.description: Italian race driver turned Paralympian Alex Zanardi has died aged 59. The former Formula 1 driver and two-time CART champion lost his legs in a horrific crash at the peak of his powers, but his drive to compete endured.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: How the Iran war is hurting travelers, airline industry
item.description: Europeans may have to reconsider their summer travel plans, as a jet fuel shortage triggered by the blockade of the Strait of Hormuz could lead airlines to set higher ticket prices.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Kosovo to hold snap elections in June
item.description: Kosovo will soon hold its third parliamentary election in a year and a half. Prime Minister Albin Kurti was able to form a government in February, but now there's political deadlock over who should be elected president.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: The significance of US military bases in Germany
item.description: President Donald Trump has said he is planning to cut US troop numbers in Germany. How many US bases are there in the country now, and what's their strategic importance?
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Ukraine plans army pay hikes, phased discharge — Zelenskyy
item.description: The Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy announced reforms to boost morale and address military complaints about exhaustion and uneven workloads on the frontline.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany needs no tips from Donald Trump, says Merz's deputy
item.description: Germany's vice chancellor says US President Donald Trump has made a mess of the Iran war and that his focus should be on ending it. As fuel prices soar, Lars Klingbeil said Trump bears responsibility for the fallout.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany news: Greens and CDU agree Baden-Württemberg deal
item.description: The state of Baden-Württemberg's Greens and conservatives have forged a coalition deal after weeks of talks. Meanwhile, unions are staging May Day rallies to demand better work conditions and a wealth tax.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Hungary: What will become of Orban and his system?
item.description: Outgoing Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban and his Fidesz party are still reeling from their election defeat earlier this month. There is much speculation about what their political future looks like.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Gender investment gap: Are women catching up with men?
item.description: Online brokers are tapping into the potential of female investors. Tailor-made products and branding campaigns are on the rise. Will this help bridge the gap between women and men when it comes to investing?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Iran's Vafaei playing for more than snooker glory
item.description: After years lost to visa battles, Hossein Vafaei is finally delivering on his early promise as a snooker prodigy. He's hoping to inspire people back home amid the turmoil in Iran.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Study busts myth: German 'spring fatigue' doesn't exist
item.description: Do we really feel more tired in spring? Researchers in Switzerland say, "there's no such thing as spring fatigue." But it is a deep cultural phenomenon in German-speaking countries.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: How widespread is global fatigue with the US?
item.description: The global image of the US has taken a blow, as many people are increasingly fed up with the constant stream of controversies surrounding the Trump administration.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: A new climate club, minus the fossil fuel lobby
item.description: Frustrated by blocking coalitions, 50-plus nations at the inaugural talks on exiting fossil fuels made one thing clear: it's all about how — not whether — to phase out coal, oil and gas.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Will EU bring in a windfall tax on oil companies?
item.description: Five countries, including Germany, want the EU to bring in a so-called windfall tax to target energy companies benefiting from the price crisis. It can be done, but it's complex.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Why UAE's OPEC exit is a blow to Saudi Arabia
item.description: The United Arab Emirates is leaving OPEC to pump more oil on its own terms. The break strips Saudi Arabia of a key partner and adds to growing uncertainty over the cartel's future.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Whale 'Timmy' reaches Denmark in rescue effort — in pictures
item.description: A barge carrying a humpback whale stranded in Germany for weeks has reached Danish waters on its journey to the North Sea.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Trump threatens US troop cuts in Germany
item.description: Donald Trump says the US is considering a "possible reduction" of its forces in Germany just days after German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said the US was being "humiliated" by Iran's leadership.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: King Charles commemorates 9/11 victims in New York visit
item.description: The royal couple laid a bouquet at the September 11 Memorial and met with victim's families.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: What are the sticking points for the EU's new budget?
item.description: The battle over the next long-term EU budget has begun. The EU's institutions have staked out their positions and are seeking significantly more funding, but it is the 27 member states that will ultimately decide.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Teenage bullying increases in Ukrainian schools
item.description: The number of teenage victims of bullying is rising in Ukrainian schools. Experts attribute this to war trauma and chronic stress among teenagers.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Russia to hold Victory Day parade without weaponry display
item.description: The scaled back parade comes amid increasing Ukrainian attacks deep into mainland Russia, including the capital Moscow. The last time a Victory Day parade was held without weaponry display was in 2007.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany: Merz hails 'historic' health care reform
item.description: The Cabinet has greenlit a health care draft law designed to lower insurance rates. The bill heads to parliament next, but doctors' associations and patient protection advocates say it amounts to austerity measures.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Football: Afghan women's team recognized in blow to Taliban
item.description: Banned from playing football and sent into exile, Afghanistan's women's team has had to fight to regain international status. That newly awarded recognition is a "hard slap to the face" of the Taliban, a player told DW.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: 2026 Goethe Medal: Meet the recipients
item.description: Though they work in different fields and countries, what unites them is their focus on a Europe in transition: Arvo Pärt, Anita Raja and Prodromos Tsinikoris have been awarded the 2026 Goethe Medal.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Why Germany wants a seat at the UN Security Council
item.description: With the rules-based world order "under stress," Germany is on a mission to gain a coveted seat at the United Nations' top table. German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul told DW why diplomacy still matters.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: UK: London stabbing being treated as 'terrorist incident'
item.description: UK police have declared the stabbing of two men in a traditionally Jewish area of London as a "terrorist incident." The victims are in a stable condition. Prime Minister Starmer called the incident "deeply concerning."
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: 'Rose' and the piece of fabric that embodies gender politics
item.description: As the film starring Sandra Hüller is released in Germany, DW talked to director Markus Schleinzer about why women passing as men inspired his historical drama, and why it's a current topic.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Hungary's Magyar meets EU's von der Leyen in high-stakes Brussels visit
item.description: Even before Peter Magyar takes office as Hungary's prime minister, he is looking to unlock €10 billion in EU funding ahead of an August deadline. Talks with Ursula von der Leyen come as part of a post-Orban EU reset.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany news: Cabinet discusses 2027 budget plans as Iran war threatens finances
item.description: The Cabinet hopes to sign off on core points of its 2027 budget, so it can start to move through parliament. Germany already plans massive borrowing next year, before adequately factoring in Iran-based costs. Follow DW.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Extreme weather and green energy on the rise in Europe
item.description: Europe's climate extremes have hit new highs — but renewables are now supplying nearly half of the continent's electricity.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: 'Only yes means yes' rape definition backed by EU lawmakers
item.description: The European Parliament voted for a consent-based definition of rape. But what constitutes rape under criminal law still varies significantly across the bloc.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: How a controversial gas pipeline deal could further stall Bosnia's EU accession
item.description: A US backed gas pipeline could help Bosnia cut reliance on Russian energy. But Brussels warns that a deal with a company linked to Trump allies could put the country's EU hopes at risk.
item.dc:subject: World

item.title: Germany's entrepreneurs betting on post-Maduro Venezuela
item.description: Venezuela's economy is opening sectors to foreign firms, and that could pay off for the few German companies that have stuck it out through years of decline.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Germany: Stranded whale 'Timmy' being transported towards ocean in special barge
item.description: Rescue teams are celebrating what they hope is a major win weeks into efforts to assist a stranded humpback whale. "Timmy" has been coaxed onto a special barge that will help steer him back towards the Atlantic.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: What Renault's new EV reveals about the global auto industry
item.description: French carmaker Renault no longer sells its brand in the Chinese market, but it's not afraid to design and develop them there. It's just one more sign of big shifts in the global auto industry.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Bundesliga games abroad unlikely, but for how long?
item.description: New proposals from FIFA will allow domestic leagues to host one game abroad a season. As a result, Europe's football leagues could face a big change sooner rather than later.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: 'Marine unicorns' aren't loving Arctic noise
item.description: Narwhals are fleeing Canada's far north. Researchers suspect a link to noise pollution from increasing ship traffic.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Germany news: Major raids carried out against Hells Angels
item.description: A large-scale police operation in the western state of North Rhine-Westphalia has targeted biker gang crime. Meanwhile, Germany is to bid again for a non-permanent seat in the UN Security Council.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Will the Iran war end Strait of Hormuz oil supremacy?
item.description: The conflict has brutally exposed the energy market's Achilles' heel. While global powers China, India and the EU push renewables, Gulf leaders are advancing plans for new bypass pipelines to safeguard their oil empires.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Germany news: Rise in young people refusing military service
item.description: Amid talk of a possible return of compulsory military service, more young Germans are applying for conscientious objector status. Meanwhile, Germany has become the world's fourth-largest military spender.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany pensions will fall short of living standards: Merz
item.description: Germany's state pension will only give "basic coverage" in retirement, Chancellor Friedrich Merz has warned amid hot debate. How does the system compare globally?
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Sawe smashes 2-hour mark setting record at London Marathon
item.description: Kenya's Sabastian Sawe has made history in London as the first man to run a marathon in under two hours. He beat the world record by more than a minute.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Queer as folk: The gay scandal that shook the German Empire
item.description: Germany's last emperor, a gilded porcelain vase and a general who had a heart attack in a pink tutu: The Eulenburg Affair shook the German Empire and Europe to its core and paved the way for the downfall of the monarchy.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Is the far-right AfD misusing its parliamentary questions?
item.description: The Alternative for Germany (AfD) has submitted thousands of official questions in state parliaments — a right of any elected party. But critics argue that their focus is meant to pressure political opponents.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: The silent power of memes
item.description: They are funny, provocative and sometimes tasteless. But memes are also powerful political tool, shaping our perception — usually without us noticing.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: German economy growth forecasts halved
item.description: Germany's economy continues to falter under the shock of soaring energy prices. In the wake of the Iran war, the German economy minister has had to cut her own forecast by half.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: States gather in a new push to ditch coal, oil and gas
item.description: At an unprecedented meeting in Colombia, delegates from more than 50 countries want to chart a practical path to wean the world off fossil fuels. Can they succeed where the UN climate talks have failed?
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Meta, Microsoft purge jobs amid AI build-up
item.description: Facebook's parent company said it would slash 10% of its workforce, while Microsoft is offering an early retirement scheme. The cuts come as both tech giants make massive investments in AI.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Germany unveils first-ever military strategy for Bundeswehr
item.description: For the first time in its history, the Bundeswehr has adopted a military strategy to prepare Germany for future threats. Large sections of the document are classified.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: The country that's turning to solar during war
item.description: Nuclear disaster is not just an abstract concept in Ukraine. It is both history and an ongoing threat that is pushing communities to turn to the sun for energy.
item.dc:subject: Climate

item.title: A book lover's dream holiday in Scotland
item.description: As UNESCO celebrates World Book Day on April 23, we visit a Scottish coastal town that was saved by books.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Bayern Munich on the brink of historic 'double treble'
item.description: With Bundesliga titles in the bag and cup finals to come, Bayern's men and women have a chance to achieve something unique in Europe. The two teams dominate in Germany and appear united in their Champions League aims.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Germany's finance minister wants to scrap spousal tax splitting
item.description: Highly controversial, but not well understood: German Finance Minister Lars Klingbeil wants to change the tax benefits for married couples.
item.dc:subject: Germany

item.title: Russia to block Kazakh oil flows to Germany via key pipeline
item.description: Germany has confirmed that Russia will block flows of Kazakh oil to a vital refinery that supplies much of Berlin's energy needs from May 1.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: The rise and fall of East Germany's Palace of the Republic
item.description: From 1976 through 1990, the building in East Berlin served as a symbol of the communist state's power. The "People's Palace" was controversially destroyed — and yet remains present.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Could Iran war trigger bigger trade crisis than COVID?
item.description: As the Iran war simmers, trade experts ask whether it could reshape supply chains more deeply than COVID-19, forcing companies to rethink strategies to counter further geopolitical shocks.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Iran war: Jet fuel crisis deepens as Lufthansa cuts flights
item.description: The impact of jet fuel shortages because of the closure of the Strait of Hormuz is starting to burn. Lufthansa has canceled 20,000 flights and experts warn more cancellations and price hikes are on the way.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: How grievance turns to violence in a mass shooter's mind
item.description: Attacks like last week's school shooting in Turkey can seem to come out of nowhere, but they rarely do. Instead, a buildup of grievance and fixation often precedes the the moment violent thoughts become violent acts.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Mars: NASA's Curiosity rover finds new organic matter in Gale Crater
item.description: NASA's Curiosity rover has identified more organic compounds as scientists investigate any potential ancient life signs on Mars. It was exploring the massive Gale Crater also believed to be a now-dry lake.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Apple switches CEO: What it means for the tech giant
item.description: Apple's top hardware engineer John Ternus will lead the $4 trillion iPhone maker, as Tim Cook steps back. Will Apple chase rivals for the smartest intelligence, or deliver the best personal AI experience?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Can data centers be green?
item.description: Renewable energy is increasingly being used to supply power-hungry data centers. As the sector swells, much of the electricity demand is being met by polluting fossil fuels.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Tim Cook to step down as Apple CEO, John Ternus to take over
item.description: John Ternus, who has long focused ​on design and ‌products at Apple, will lead the iPhone maker as it gears up for an industry change spurred by artificial intelligence.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Bundesliga debut by female coach marred by misogyny
item.description: Marie-Louise Eta's historic debut as a head coach in the men's Bundesliga ended in defeat. While her appointment is a sign of progress, the reactions to it leave no doubt about how much is still to be done.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: China's chip ambitions shake up global tech industry
item.description: US curbs on advanced chips pushed China to focus on its own semiconductor ecosystem. And while it trails at the very cutting edge, China's "good‑enough" technology is fast powering much of the global economy.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Mines in the Strait of Hormuz: How dangerous are they?
item.description: Iran says it has placed mines in the Straight of Hormuz to discourage independent traffic. How dangerous are naval mines, and what can be done to clear them?
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: Climate change impacts India's harvest festivals
item.description: As people across India celebrate traditional agrarian spring festivals, climate change has become an unwanted guest at the table. How are communities rising to the pressure on harvests, water, and rural life?
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Hannover Messe 2026: Brazilian flair awaits visitors
item.description: With war in Iran on hold, the world's largest industrial trade fair opens in Hannover. And Brazil, this year's partner country, is pulling out all stops to convince visitors it's a reliable technology leader.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: The German who recovered from a stroke to seal Olympic first
item.description: Kathrin Marchand was an elite rower for years before becoming a doctor. But a stroke at the age of 30 changed everything. Despite the challenges, she has now achieved something no other Olympian ever has.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Record fuel prices: How are governments responding?
item.description: In Germany, a liter of diesel costs up to 40% more than before the Iran war and other countries have seen even bigger price jumps. What are governments around the world doing to combat record fuel prices?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Teen discovers first ancient Greek artifact found in Berlin
item.description: A 13-year-old stumbled upon a coin from ancient Troy — an extremely rare find in Germany. Experts say it may offer new clues about little-known links between ancient Greece and northern Europe.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Chess: Uzbekistan's new star shows Asia's continued rise
item.description: Javokhir Sindarov of Uzbekistan has earned the right to challenge India's Dommaraju Gukesh for the world title. Both men are under 21 and from Asia, underlining an ongoing demographic shift in top level chess.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Nueva Germania: The failed 'Aryan project' in Paraguay
item.description: A new home for the "Aryan race." That was what German emigrants envisioned when they founded Nueva Germania in Paraguay in 1886. It failed back then but a version of the town still exists today.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Senegal: The Goethe-Institut opens its new building in Dakar
item.description: The Goethe-Institut's new building in Dakar champions sustainable architecture, made from the earth. Star architect Francis Kere has blended local materials with contemporary design.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Anatomy of the clitoris: 3D images reveal its nerve system
item.description: New 3D images reveal the clitoris’s complex nerve network, highlighting long‑standing gaps in medical understanding of the female body.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: US jury finds Live Nation, Ticketmaster hold illegal monopoly
item.description: The verdict will not bring immediate relief to concertgoers frustrated by high ticket prices, but could cost the company hundreds of millions of dollars.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: World Cup 2026: Fans with disabilities 'excluded'
item.description: Complaints that the 2026 World Cup, hosted largely by the USA, is a rip off have been widespread and constant. But it's not just the prices that are making it feel impossible for football fans with disabilities.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Solar power in Morocco's desert: Bold vision, mixed results
item.description: A massive solar tower in the Moroccan desert is the beacon of an ambitious push for a clean energy future. But fossil fuels and grid constraints stand in the way.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Women's workwear: When proper fit becomes a safety issue
item.description: With limited options, many women in trades and industry have to wear men's workwear. But a poor fit causes discomfort and can compromise safety. Now, some firms are stepping up to design safer, better-fitting gear.
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: What we call sex work — and what it says about society
item.description: A Bonn exhibition traces the cultural history of sex work and shows how the words used to describe it have shaped stigma, policy and lived experience.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: The world ditched wasteful toilets, the US stayed behind
item.description: US toilets use far more water than many of their global counterparts. President Donald Trump is pushing to loosen water pressure standards, a move critics say would increase waste.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Lufthansa pilots launch 2-day strike, cabin crew say they will strike right after
item.description: Pilots who are members of the Vereinigung Cockpit trade union are striking on Monday and Tuesday. As hundreds of flights were canceled, the UFO cabin crew union said it would also strike on Wednesday and Thursday.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Trump's Strait of Hormuz blockade hits Iran's oil trade
item.description: A US naval blockade cuts off Iran's main source of hard currency by halting most of its oil exports. Will President Trump's pressure force Tehran back to negotiations or widen the conflict?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Merck's Keytruda: A lifesaving drug, a global divide
item.description: A cross-border investigation by DW and ICIJ reveals how pricing and patents helped turn a life-saving medicine into one of the world's best selling drugs while leaving many patients struggling to access it.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: UFOs: 'Most are reported by people out for an evening smoke'
item.description: Hansjürgen Köhler has been a volunteer UFO hunter for more than 50 years. He sees his work as scientific investigation, but sometimes also as caring for others.
item.dc:subject: Science

item.title: First gay rights movement: Berlin's wild 1920s queer history
item.description: Before the Nazis took power, Berlin was a center of LGBTQ+ rights and queer culture, with research institutes, a flourishing nightlife and one of the earliest gay rights' movements.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: NASA's billion-dollar space race goes into overdrive
item.description: NASA's renewed push to get a human mission to the moon and Mars is neither cheap, nor for the faint of heart. But who ends up covering the costs and what does everyone still on Earth get out of all this?
item.dc:subject: Business

item.title: Recycling's next big thing — or big bluff?
item.description: An advanced recycling process claims it can handle hard-to-recycle plastics, like packaging. But critics say it's not worth the effort.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Has Coachella festival sold out?
item.description: One of the world's biggest open-air music and art festivals has been hit by controversies and declining credibility — yet the world's biggest acts continue to headline.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Veteran German actor Mario Adorf dies aged 95
item.description: Few actors had a career as rich as Mario Adorf. He played legendary Hollywood villains, wrote German TV history and still had film roles at age 90.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Artemis II crew emotional as mission prepares for reentry
item.description: In less than 48 hours, the Orion spacecraft is due to splash down in the Pacific Ocean. Crew members have said what they saw in space will forever change how they see the world.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: Hungary election: How football has helped Orban keep power
item.description: Viktor Orban is a football fanatic but has long used the sport as a political tool, both in Hungary and abroad. With polls predicting election defeat, he could also lose his chance of hosting the Champions League final.
item.dc:subject: Sports

item.title: Norway's Telenor faces lawsuit for giving junta data from its customers in Myanmar
item.description: A Swedish non-profit has filed a class action lawsuit against Norway's Telenor, accusing it of endangering customers in Myanmar by sharing their data with the junta. The company said it had no real choice.
item.dc:subject: News

item.title: How farmers are rising to a fertilizer shortfall
item.description: The war in Iran has sparked a global fertilizer shortage and is threatening food security. What are farmers around the world doing to save their crops?
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: Should Western influencers promote Taliban‑run Afghanistan?
item.description: In the race for clicks, likes and followers, some travel content creators are being criticized for promoting Afghanistan while ignoring the harsh reality of Taliban rule.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: 60 years after the Indonesian mass killings: Is the Cold War back?
item.description: Six decades after helping the Indonesian army crush one of the biggest communist parties in the world, the US is once again meddling in countries it considers a thorn in its flesh. Is history repeating itself?
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Homegrown energy is slashing US energy bills
item.description: In times of skyrocketing energy prices, some US citizens are making big savings by producing electricity at home.
item.dc:subject: Environment

item.title: One in five young Germans plan to leave the country
item.description: A recent study shows that as many as one in five Germans between the ages of 14 and 29 are actively thinking about moving to another country — and 40% plan to do so in the long term.
item.dc:subject: Culture

item.title: Mediterranean sharks are vanishing in a legal void
item.description: Along Libya's coast, sharks — including breeding females — are being sold unchecked. With little enforcement and growing financial pressure on fishermen, vulnerable species are spiraling toward extinction.
item.dc:subject: World
